beats.text: When two classes are linearly separable, infinitely many lines split them, so the question is which one is best.

visual.sub: Pick the boundary with the widest empty buffer

beats.text: A support vector machine picks the boundary that keeps the largest possible empty corridor on either side.
A hyperplane is the set of points where w dot x plus b equals zero, and the prediction is the sign of that score.

visual.note: w is the normal vector across the boundary

beats.text: The geometric margin is the smallest signed distance from the boundary to any point, and the SVM maximizes it.

visual.note: labels are plus or minus one so the label cancels the sign

beats.text: Under canonical scaling the margin collapses to one over the norm of w, so maximizing margin means minimizing that norm.
The generalization bound degrades with one over gamma squared, not with dimension, so wide margins tolerate huge feature spaces.

concepts.title: The hard-margin problem and its support vectors

beats.text: With the redundancy pinned by canonical scaling, the SVM becomes a clean convex optimization problem.

visual.sub: A quadratic program with one global optimum

beats.text: Minimize half the squared norm of w subject to every point sitting at least one margin-unit from the boundary.
In the dual the data enters only through inner products, and the Lagrange multipliers are zero for points off the margin.
Only the points on the margin carry a nonzero multiplier, and those points are the support vectors.
On a clean problem a trained SVM might be a dozen support vectors out of a million training points.

visual.label: support vectors out of one million points

beats.text: If the norm of w is two, the canonical margin is one half, since doubling the norm halves the corridor.

visual.label: margin gamma when norm of w is two

concepts.title: Soft margin and the hinge loss

beats.text: Real data overlaps, so the hard-margin problem often has no feasible solution at all.

visual.sub: Let constraints break, at a price

beats.text: Cortes and Vapnik added a slack variable per point and paid for the total slack with a penalty C.

visual.note: C sets the exchange rate between margin width and violations

beats.text: Large C punishes violations harshly toward low bias, while small C tolerates mistakes for a fatter margin.
Eliminating the slack recasts the SVM as regularized risk minimization with one loss, the hinge.

visual.note: zero once correctly classified with margin at least one

beats.text: A point classified well clear of the margin at signed margin one point two costs nothing.
A point on the wrong side at signed margin minus zero point five costs one point five, growing linearly the deeper it strays.

concepts.title: The kernel trick: RBF and polynomial

beats.text: A flat hyperplane is useless for concentric rings, so the classic escape is to lift the data into higher dimensions.

visual.sub: Bend the boundary without visiting the lifted space

beats.text: The SVM never needs the feature map itself, only inner products, so a cheap function that equals them suffices.

visual.note: swap every dot product for K and nothing else changes

beats.text: Mercer's theorem says any symmetric kernel with a positive semi-definite Gram matrix corresponds to some valid feature map.
The decision function becomes a weighted vote of support vectors through the kernel, never touching coordinates.

visual.note: the linear SVM becomes a universal nonlinear classifier

beats.text: The RBF kernel is a smooth bump of similarity that decays with distance, and its feature map is infinite-dimensional.

visual.note: small gamma reaches far, large gamma is hyper-local

beats.text: Width gamma is decisive: small gamma gives a smooth boundary, large gamma wraps tightly around individual points.

concepts.title: SVMs in practice — and against the field

beats.text: From the late 1990s until 2012, kernel SVMs were the most accurate general-purpose classifiers available.

visual.sub: When they win, and why they faded

beats.text: Scaling features is not optional, because both the dot product and the RBF distance are dominated by large-magnitude columns.
Start with the RBF kernel and grid-search C and gamma jointly on a log scale with cross-validation.
If nearly every training point becomes a support vector, gamma is too large or the problem is too noisy.
RBF training cost grows roughly between N squared and N cubed, which rules it out for the largest datasets.
The kernel idea never died: it reappears in Gaussian processes and in the attention mechanism, a learned similarity kernel.

visual.label: year deep nets passed kernel SVMs on perception
